summary: Jeff Atwood argues that regular expressions are unfairly maligned due to a misunderstood Jamie Zawinski quote, which actually warns against overuse rather than use entirely. He contends that regular expressions are powerful tools that belong in every programmer's toolkit when used with restraint. The post demonstrates that regex readability is a skill issue, not an inherent flaw, showing how whitespace, comments, and proper tooling can make complex patterns comprehensible. Atwood provides practical tips including breaking complex patterns into smaller ones, using whitespace liberally, leveraging dedicated regex tools, and understanding that regex cannot replace proper parsers. He concludes that with responsible use and appropriate tooling, regular expressions offer significant wins in writing concise, powerful code.
key_points: The famous Zawinski quote is misunderstood - it warns against overuse of regex, not regex itself
Unreadable regular expressions are a skill and discipline problem, not an inherent limitation of the technology
Modern regex features like whitespace mode and comments can make patterns highly readable
Dedicated regex tools with syntax highlighting and real-time testing are essential for working effectively with patterns
Regular expressions are not parsers and should not be used for tasks requiring balanced tag matching
money_quotes: Regular expressions are like a particularly spicy hot sauce – to be used in moderation and with restraint only when appropriate.
If you read an incredibly complex, impossible to decipher regular expression in your codebase, they did it wrong.
Writing so that people can understand you is hard. I don't care if it's code, English, regular expressions, or Klingon.
You wouldn't read or write code in notepad, right? Then why in the world would you attempt to read or write regular expressions that way?
Do not try to do everything in one uber-regex. I know you can do it that way, but you're not going to. It's not worth it.
No matter how clever your regex, don't delude yourself: it is in no way, shape or form a substitute for a real live parser.
If you make regular expressions a part of your toolkit, you'll be able to write less code that does more.
key_insight: Regular expressions aren't inherently unreadable or evil; they become problematic only through overuse, poor formatting, and lack of proper tooling - the same factors that make any code difficult to maintain.
